region_of_origin: Egypt and the Levant (Egypt, Lebanon, Israel all claim origin); widely available in Singapore at halal shawarma and Mediterranean food stalls
food_subgroup: Deep-fried legume-based patties; chickpea and broad bean fritters with herbs and spices

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for falafel as a specific food item in available clinical terminology resources.

allergens: sesame (possible — tahini sometimes added to mixture)

serving.description: 3 pieces (~75g cooked) — standard Singapore shawarma shop portion
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of falafel in Singapore shawarma shops is 3 pieces weighing approximately 75g cooked, providing around 218 kcal, 10g protein, 13g fat, and 17g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Falafel — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174281)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 174281 is a direct match for deep-fried falafel. All per-100g nutrient values are taken from this record. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown was not separately reported in the input record; USDA FDC 174281 does report these but they were not included in the input data — see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; chickpea-based foods GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for deep-fried falafel exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) boiled chickpeas GI ~28–35 (University of Sydney GI Database, multiple entries); (2) frying fat slows gastric emptying and lowers postprandial glucose response; (3) peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) supports chickpea-based foods as low-GI (GI 28–45 range). GL per 75g serving = (40 × 17g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.8; input record states 5.6 using slightly lower available carbohydrate estimate (~14g net). GL reported as 5.6 per input source consistent with ~14g net carbohydrate after fibre subtraction. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~300mg per 75g serving); potassium-rich chickpea base partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: A 75g serving of falafel (3 pieces) contains approximately 300mg sodium — low for a cooked appetiser — and 263mg potassium, giving a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; individuals with hypertension can include falafel as a starter provided accompanying sauces (tahini, garlic sauce) and pita bread are accounted for in total daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load per serving (~5.6); high dietary fibre (5.5g/100g) slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 40 and a GL of ~5.6 per 3-piece serving, falafel is one of the most glycaemically favourable fried foods available; the chickpea base provides 5.5g dietary fibre per 100g which further attenuates postprandial glucose response, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (2.1g/100g), high plant protein and folate; frying oil contributes unsaturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Falafel contains no cholesterol, only 2.1g saturated fat per 100g, and provides 90mcg folate and 3.5mg iron per 100g from the chickpea base; the absorbed frying oil is predominantly unsaturated, and the high fibre content (5.5g/100g) supports LDL cholesterol reduction, making falafel a heart-favourable choice among fried street foods when consumed in standard 3-piece portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herb profile (parsley, coriander, garlic allicin); high folate and iron; zero cholesterol
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory herbs integral to falafel (parsley, coriander, cumin, garlic) provide flavonoids and allicin compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties; combined with 90mcg folate and 3.5mg iron per 100g, falafel is a nutritionally positive choice for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Falafel are deep-fried Middle Eastern patties made from ground chickpeas and/or broad beans (fava beans) blended with onion, garlic, parsley, coriander, cumin, and salt, then shaped into balls or discs and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp on the outside with a dense, herb-flecked interior. Originating in Egypt and the Levant (with Egypt, Lebanon, and Israel all claiming the dish), falafel is a staple street food and mezze item widely available in Singapore at halal shawarma shops, Middle Eastern restaurants, and Mediterranean food stalls, typically served as 3 pieces (~75g) as a starter or inside a pita wrap. Despite being deep-fried, falafel has a surprisingly favourable nutritional profile: per 100g it provides 290 kcal, 13.3g protein, 17.8g fat (predominantly unsaturated from absorbed frying oil), 22.7g carbohydrate, 5.5g dietary fibre, and 400mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 40 (low), yielding a glycemic load of ~5.6 per 75g serving — making it one of the most glycaemically favourable fried foods available. The chickpea base is an excellent source of folate (90mcg/100g), plant iron (3.5mg/100g), and potassium (350mg/100g). Falafel is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease in standard portions. Nutrient data sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174281.
meal_context: Singapore hawker centre Middle Eastern stall

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174281, an approved primary source with an exact match for deep-fried falafel. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chickpea entries and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008), with confidence 0.9. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved source.
notes: 1. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown was not included in the input record's per_100g data. USDA FDC 174281 does report these values; a future revision should extract them directly. 2. Water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the input record. 3. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC 174281 for this preparation. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; falafel is a vegan food with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as falafel is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea-base GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for deep-fried falafel was found. 5. Sodium value of 400mg/100g from USDA FDC 174281 equates to approximately 300mg per 75g serving. Commercial Singapore shawarma shop falafel may vary. 6. The primary alias '29. Falafel' from the input record has been cleaned to 'Falafel' as the canonical primary alias; the numbered prefix was a data artefact. 7. Falafel is classified under 'Meat and alternatives' food group as a high-protein legume-based dish serving as a protein source in the meal.
